display_name: IPv6 next-hop/host unreachable — ND not resolving
entry_symptom: An IPv6 next-hop or on-link host is unreachable: the neighbor entry is INCOMPLETE or missing, traffic to it is black-holed, while the interface is up. (Use this when IPv6 ND fails to resolve a neighbor.)

nodes.question: Does the interface have a valid IPv6 link-local (fe80::) address and is IPv6 enabled? (ND sources every NS/NA/RS from the link-local; without it nothing resolves.)

nodes.finding: No usable link-local address — IPv6 ND cannot operate on the interface, so every neighbor will appear unresolved.
nodes.action: Enable IPv6 on the interface (e.g. 'ipv6 enable' or configure a global address to derive a link-local). If the link-local itself shows DUPLICATE/DAD-failed, resolve the fe80:: duplicate (often a duplicate MAC across redundancy groups) and bounce the interface. On SONiC, verify IPv6 is up on the port and link-local mode where required ('show ipv6 interfaces', 'show ipv6 link-local-mode').

nodes.question: What state is the neighbor cache entry for the target IPv6 address in?
The neighbor is stuck INCOMPLETE (NS sent, no NA). Is ICMPv6 ND (types 135/136) permitted on the path, and is the target host up on the same VLAN/segment?

nodes.finding: The neighbor resolves (REACHABLE/STALE/DELAY/PROBE with a MAC) — ND itself is healthy. STALE is normal and is NOT a failure; the cached MAC is still used to forward.
nodes.action: Stop investigating ND. Look beyond it: routing/next-hop selection, ACLs on the forwarding path, MTU/PMTUD (ICMPv6 Packet Too Big filtering), or hardware forwarding programming. On SONiC confirm the neighbor is also in ASIC_DB ('redis-cli -n 1 KEYS "ASIC_STATE:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_NEIGHBOR_ENTRY*" | grep <ipv6>').

nodes.finding: The address failed Duplicate Address Detection (DUPLICATE) — it is NOT assigned, and if it is the interface link-local, IPv6 on the interface is disabled (RFC 4862 5.4.5).
nodes.action: Find the conflicting node (another device with the same address, a duplicate virtual MAC for HSRPv6/VRRPv3, or a re-used static address). Rule out an L2 loop reflecting the node's own DAD NS. Resolve the duplicate, then re-run DAD by bouncing the interface or reconfiguring the address. IOS-XR: 'show ipv6 nd dadfail' lists DAD-failed addresses; SONiC: 'ip -6 address show dev <ifname>' shows the 'dadfailed' flag.

nodes.finding: ICMPv6 ND messages are being dropped by an ACL or control-plane policy. ND is ICMPv6 — a 'deny icmp' ported from IPv4 kills neighbor and router discovery (and type 2 Packet Too Big filtering also breaks IPv6 PMTUD).
nodes.action: Explicitly permit ICMPv6 ND types 133,134,135,136 (and 137 if redirects are used) plus type 2 (Packet Too Big) before any ICMPv6 deny, and permit link-local (fe80::/10) and solicited-node multicast (ff02::1:ff00:0/104). Re-test resolution and large-flow PMTUD after the change. SONiC: ensure control-plane/data ACLs permit ICMPv6 (IP protocol 58) ND types.

nodes.finding: ICMPv6 is permitted but the neighbor still will not resolve — the target host is down, is on a different VLAN/SVI than ND is sent on, or the solicited-node multicast (ff02::1:ffXX:XXXX) is not being delivered (MLD-snooping pruning or the node never joined the group).
nodes.action: Confirm the host is up and on the same L2 segment; verify the SVI/VLAN mapping; check the MAC is learned in the L2 table; and verify MLD-snooping is not pruning the solicited-node multicast. Ping the target to force re-resolution and watch the cache leave INCOMPLETE.

nodes.finding: SONiC-specific: the Linux kernel resolved the neighbor (present in STATE_DB NEIGH_TABLE) but orchagent did not program the SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_NEIGHBOR_ENTRY into ASIC_DB, so hardware drops the traffic despite a healthy software cache.
nodes.action: Compare kernel vs ASIC: 'redis-cli -n 6 HGETALL "NEIGH_TABLE|<ifname>|<ipv6>"' (kernel) against 'redis-cli -n 1 KEYS "ASIC_STATE:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_NEIGHBOR_ENTRY*" | grep <ipv6>' (ASIC). If the ASIC entry is missing, check 'docker logs swss' for SAI neighbor errors; a swss restart forces a resync (disruptive — brief traffic impact).

sources.title: RFC 4861 — Neighbor Discovery for IPv6 (neighbor cache state machine)
